coredata.dc:title: Functional carbon dots induced defect configuration entropy strengthening polyanion cathode for ultrafast-charging sodium ion batteries in a wide temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yujin
Mei, Yu
Liu, Huaxin
Ding, Hanrui
Huang, Yujie
Zhong, Xue
Geng, Zhenglei
He, Zidong
Deng, Wentao
Zou, Guoqiang
Ji, Xiaobo
Hou, Hongshuai
coredata.dc:description: The development of fast-charging and wide-temperature sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) is of great significance to all-climate energy storage. However, the gigantic challenge lies in achieving super dynamic performance and stability of cathode materials. Herein, the defect configuration entropy (DCE) is conceptualized in Na3V2(PO4)3/carbon composite (NVP/C) cathode by manipulating heteroatoms and vacancies in the surface carbon layer. It is found that the bonding energy between the carbon layer and NVP interface increases with the DCE. Accompanying, the Na+ migration energy barrier is reduced and the electronic conductivity is enhanced, which are pivotal factors underpinning the low-temperature performance. Furthermore, the increase of DCE engenders the formation of a NaF-rich cathode-electrolyte interface, furnishing support for high-temperature stability. As a result, the DCE-strengthened NVP/carbon composite cathode exhibits a high capacity of ∼88 mAh g−1 at an ultrahigh rate of 200 C (13 s per charge process). Moreover, the superior electrochemical performance in the wide temperature range of −30–60 °C is achieved with 0.101 % capacity decay per cycle at −20 ℃, and 0.0047 % capacity decay per cycle at 60 ℃.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium-ion batteries
Polyanionic cathodes
Carbon dots
Defect configuration entropy
Fast-charging batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DFT calculations. a-d) Carbon layer models with different doping elements. e-h) Composite models of the four designed carbon layers with NVP cathodes. i-l) pDOS and COHP calculations of V-C bonds in NVP/C, NVP/FC, NVP/NSFC, and NVP/NSFC-V. m-p) pDOS and COHP calculations of V-O bonds in NVP/C, NVP/FC, NVP/NSFC, and NVP /NSFC-V. q) Schematic illustration of the inter-layer migration of Na+ for NVP/NSFC. r) Schematic illustration of the bulk migration of Na+ for NVP/NSFC. s) Migration energy barriers for Na+ migration in the interlayer. t) Migration energy barriers for Na+ migration in the cathode bulk phase.

a-c) Rietveld refinement of the XRD patterns of NVP/C series samples. d) Raman spectra of NVP/C samples. High-resolution XPS spectra of e) C 1 s and f) V 2p for the NVP/C samples. SEM images of g) NVP/C, h) NVP/FC and i) NVP/NSFC. HRTEM images of j) NVP/C, k) NVP/FC and l) NVP/NSFC. IFFT images and the lattice spacing of m) NVP/C, o) NVP/FC and q) NVP/NSFC. SEAD images of n) NVP/C, p) NVP/FC and r) NVP/NSFC. EDS elemental mapping of s) NVP/C, t) NVP/FC and u) NVP/NSFC.

Electrochemical performances of the NVP/C samples. CV curves at different scanning rates of a) NVP/C, b) NVP/FC, and c) NVP/NSFC. d) Nyquist plots of the NVP/C series. e) Rate performances of NVP/C samples from 0.5 to 100 C. f) Comparison of the rate performances of NVP/NSFC half cells to the previous results in the literature. g) Long-term cycling performance of NVP/NSFC at the rate of 100 C. h) Low-temperature performance of NVP/C series samples at −20 °C. i) High-temperature performance of NVP/C series samples at 60 °C.

a) GCD curves of NVP/NSFC. b) In-situ XRD patterns of NVP/NSFC electrode during the extraction/intercalation of Na+ for (113), (024), (211), and (116) crystal plane. XRD diffraction peak displacement changes corresponding to the (113) crystal plane of the NVP/NSFC for c) discharging at 2.3 V and d) charging at 4.0 V. e-h) XANES spectra of V K-edge for NVP/C series cathodes at pristine and different charge/discharge states. i) EXAFS R space curves of V K-edge. j) Schematic of NVP anodic VO6 octahedral first shell layer coordination. EXAFS wavelet transforms images of V K-edge for k) NVP/C, l) NVP/FC, and m) NVP/NSFC.

Electrode surface properties and CEI chemical composition. Contact angle of a) NVP/C, b) NVP/FC, and c) NVP/NSFC after dropping a liquid electrolyte for different times at 25 °C. d-f) High-resolution etch XPS measurements of C 1 s orbital for NVP/C series electrodes after 1000 cycles. g-i) High-resolution etch XPS measurements of F 1 s orbital for NVP/C series electrodes after 1000 cycles. TOF-SIMS measurements: 3D reconstruction of NaCl2 -, NaO-, NaF2 -, NaCO3 - fragments for j1-j4) NVP/C, k1-k4) NVP/FC, and l1-l4) NVP/NSFC electrodes after 1000 cycles.

Electrochemical behavior of NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cells. a) Schematic illustration of NVP/NSFC ‖ HC. b) Photos of a lighted sign lit by the NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cell. c) GCD curves of NVP/NSFC cathode and hard carbon anode. d) Long-term Cycling performance of NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cell at 5 C for 1400 cycles. e) Rate performance of NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cell. f) Comparison of the electrochemical performances of NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cell to the previous results in the literatures. g) Long-term cycling performances at 10 C for 1500 cycles.

head.ce:title: Functional carbon dots induced defect configuration entropy strengthening polyanion cathode for ultrafast-charging sodium ion batteries in a wide temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of fast-charging and wide-temperature sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) is of great significance to all-climate energy storage. However, the gigantic challenge lies in achieving super dynamic performance and stability of cathode materials. Herein, the defect configuration entropy (DCE) is conceptualized in Na3V2(PO4)3/carbon composite (NVP/C) cathode by manipulating heteroatoms and vacancies in the surface carbon layer. It is found that the bonding energy between the carbon layer and NVP interface increases with the DCE. Accompanying, the Na+ migration energy barrier is reduced and the electronic conductivity is enhanced, which are pivotal factors underpinning the low-temperature performance. Furthermore, the increase of DCE engenders the formation of a NaF-rich cathode-electrolyte interface, furnishing support for high-temperature stability. As a result, the DCE-strengthened NVP/carbon composite cathode exhibits a high capacity of ∼88 mAh g−1 at an ultrahigh rate of 200 C (13 s per charge process). Moreover, the superior electrochemical performance in the wide temperature range of −30–60 °C is achieved with 0.101 % capacity decay per cycle at −20 ℃, and 0.0047 % capacity decay per cycle at 60 ℃.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: To meet the requirement of all-climates sodium ion batteries, it is crucial to develop fast-charging cathode that can work in a wide-temperature environment (-30 ℃-60 ℃). A new concept of defect configuration entropy is proposed to construct robust polyanion/carbon composite cathode materials. The defect configuration entropy-strengthened cathode shows extraordinary interface and bulk stability, ultra-fast dynamics and beneficial CEI components.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As a key supporting technology for the energy revolution, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used in 3 C products and electric vehicles for their high energy density, no memory effect, low self-discharge, long cycle life, and environmental friendliness [1,2]. Nonetheless, the finite and unequally distributed lithium resources and high costs pose significant constraints on meeting the burgeoning demands of large-scale applications [3]. Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) operate on a similar principle to LIBs. However, the abundant and economically viable sodium resources present a promising alternative for SIBs as an ideal counterpart to LIBs, particularly in facilitating large-scale integration of renewable energy sources and energy storage [4]. To meet the requirements of energy storage under all climate conditions, it is crucial to develop SIBs that can operate in wide-temperature environments (-30 °C −60 °C) [5].

The pivotal advancement of wide-temperature SIBs is intricately entwined with the ongoing exploration of electrode materials, with cathode materials playing a crucial role in achieving comprehensive performance benchmarks [6,7]. The challenge lies in achieving both dynamic performance and stability of cathode materials [8,9]. Among the pantheon of contenders, layered transition metal oxides generally exhibit high specific capacity, however, they face limitations such as phase transition and degradation during cycling and low thermal stability [10]. Prussian blue analogs offer the advantages of low cost and stable structure. Nevertheless, they suffer from low energy density and the drawback of excessive crystal water, which can complicate processing [11]. The polyanionic sodium superior conductor epitomized by sodium vanadium phosphate Na3V2(PO4)3 (NVP) emerges as a beacon of promise [12]. Boasting a relatively high-voltage platform (3.4 V vs. Na/Na+) and theoretical energy density, NVP is hailed as one of the most auspicious cathode materials for SIBs. Its three-dimensional framework structure, fortified by robust P-O covalent bonds, imbues NVP with commendable attributes including sodium ion migration kinetics, thermal resilience, and structural steadfastness [13]. However, the nuanced challenge arises from the inherent isolating property of the PO4 3- group, stemming from its voluminous size, which engenders low electronic conductivity and constricted capacity in NVP [14]. Research endeavors have underscored the efficacy of carbon composites in ameliorating this conundrum [15,16]. While, traditional carbon materials have fewer sites capable of interacting with NVP, making it difficult to form stable composites and leading to poor electrochemical performance [17,18].

High-entropy materials are a type of emerging materials composed of multiple components [19]. By combining multiple principal elements, it is possible to maximize configurational entropy and obtain robust properties [20]. Inspired by the concept of entropy stabilization in metal alloys, this strategy has rapidly expanded to systems used for energy storage [21,22]. Encouraged by the conventional high-entropy strategies, in this work we propose the concept of defect configuration entropy to resolve the bonding sites shortage of carbon layer/NVP. The five heteroatoms (H, O, N, S, F) along with carbon vacancies, are progressively introduced to increase the defect-configuration entropy of the carbon layer. Interestingly, as the defect-configuration entropy increases, the bonding energy between the carbon layer and NVP interface strengthens. Simultaneously, the reduction in the Na+ migration energy barrier and the augmentation of electronic conductivity emerged as pivotal factors underpinning the superlative fast-charging performance, laying a foundation for low-temperature performance. Furthermore, the defective heterogeneous interface engenders the formation of a NaF-rich cathode-electrolyte interface (CEI), furnishing support for high-temperature stability. Consequently, defect configuration entropy-strengthened NVP/carbon composite (NVP/NSFC) exhibited an exceptional specific capacity of 88.9 mAh g−1 at 200 C and a remarkable capacity retention rate of 90.2 % following 5000 cycles at 100 C. Moreover, outstanding electrochemical performance spanned a wide-operational temperature of −30°C to 60°C, retaining 69.6 % capacity after 300 stable cycles at −20 ℃ (1 C), and preserving 92.9 % capacity after 1500 cycles at 60 ℃ (10 C). Finally, the NVP/NSFC ‖ hard carbon (HC) full cell exhibited a capacity retention rate of 93.1 % after 1400 cycles at 5 C. Collectively, the implementation of defect configuration entropy heralds a promising strategy for unlocking the full potential of high-performance polyanionic cathodes, propelling them towards a bright future in practical applications.

A series of heterogeneous carbon layers with different defect configurations were designed by rationally regulating the type and number of defects. The devised carbon layers were composited with NVP cathodes to obtain the corresponding NVP/C composite cathode materials. Among them, the bare carbon layer ( Fig. 1a and Figure S1a) is composed of C/H/O elements, whereas the F-doped carbon layer (Fig. 1b and Figure S1b) consists of C/H/O/F elements, and the N/S/F co-doped carbon layer (Fig. 1c and Figure S1c) incorporated C/H/O/F/N/S elements. Considering that during the sintering process of NVP preparation, F atoms are easy to volatilize at high temperatures, especially when it exceeds 400 °C [23]. On the one hand, F-containing compounds will decompose at high temperatures to form volatile by-products, resulting in the loss of the F atom. On the other hand, F atoms may react with other components to form volatile compounds, which further aggravates the loss of F [24]. Subsequently, the loss of F atoms will cause defects in the carbon material, such as vacancies and point defects [25]. Hence, the emergence of vacancy defects is inevitable within F-doped carbon layers or N/S/F co-doped carbon layers. To present a comprehensive comparison, the model with the coexistence of H/O/N/S/F/vacancy was added (Fig. 1d and Figure S1d). If the influence of other configurations on the entropy is not counted, which is acceptable, the entropy of the system is dominated by the configurational entropy of the atomic arrangement. The defect configuration of the carbon layer increases with the induction of heterogeneous elements and defect types [26]. The configurational entropy (ΔS config) value can be obtained by the following equation under ideal conditions where the system components are randomly mixed:[27]
(1)
Δ
S
cof
= − R
∑
i = 1
n
c
i
I n
c
i

Where R is the gas constant, n is the number of group elements, and c
i is the molar fraction of component i. It can be seen from the formula that for an ideal solid solution, the more the number of components, the closer the component content, and the higher the mixing entropy. Thus, four distinct groups of carbon layers are identified with a gradual increase in defect configuration entropy. The composite models featuring the gradient entropy effect with NVP cathodes are illustrated in Fig. 1e-h. The intrinsic stability of the composite cathode, influenced by the entropic effects of various defect configurations, has been initially appraised through density functional theory (DFT) computations. As the structural stability of the cathode is directly affected by the stability of the active element in the bulk phase, the vacancy formation energy of vanadium (V) in NVP composites was compared (Figure S2). A direct correlation between the enhancement of defect configuration entropy and the increased formation energy of V vacancies is revealed. Interestingly, the increase in defect configuration entropy inhibits the formation of V-vacancies (Figure S3 and Table S1). Notably, NVP/NSFC-vacancy (NVP/NSFC-V) exhibits the highest vacancy formation energy, thus demonstrating superior structural stability compared to NVP/C, which displays the lowest vacancy formation energy.

Subsequently, the bonding capacity between interfaces was investigated, especially the V-C and V-O bonds (Fig. 1i-p). The peak resonance (overlap) of pDOS illustrates the interaction region of V with C/O, while the combined COHP reflects the bonding interactions belonging to bonding or antibonding states in different energy ranges. An increase in the -iCOHP value indicates enhanced binding, it is evident that the defect configuration effect amplifies the bonding capacity of V-C, thereby enhancing the interfacial stability between the carbon layer and NVP cathode (Table S2). Likewise, the bonding capacity of V-O gradually strengthens from NVP/C to NVP/NSFC. However, with the emergence of vacancy defects in the carbon layer, there is a partial decrease in V-O bond strength. It is conjectured that the introduction of vacancy defects in the carbon layer leads to a stronger interaction between the V in the NVP and the carbon layer, thereby causing a certain weakening of the electronic interactions between the V and O in the NVP (Table S3). Nevertheless, taking the results of the V vacancy formation energy into account, it becomes apparent that the predominant trend is the enhancement of the defect configuration entropy effect, which ultimately bolsters the stability of V at the interface.

The influence of defect-configuration entropy effect on the electronic and ionic conductivity of the NVP cathode is further scrutinized. The total density of states (tDOS) calculations speculate that the NVP/NSFC-V composite obtains better electronic conductivity (Figure S4). The Na+ diffusion in the interlayer between the carbon layer and the NVP cathode (Fig. 1q and Figure S5a-c), as well as the Na+ diffusion in the bulk phase of NVP (Fig. 1r and Figure S5d-f) were calculated separately. As depicted in Fig. 1s, diffusion energy barrier values of NVP/C, NVP/FC, NVP/NSFC, and NVP/NSFC-V are 0.790, 0.748, 0.675, and 0.581 eV, respectively. This indicates that the enhancement of the defect configuration entropy effect significantly improves the ion diffusion ability of the interlayer. The Na+ diffusion in the bulk phase mirrors the interlayer diffusion trend, albeit with a minor reduction in the energy barrier for NVP/NSFC-V compared to NVP/NSFC (Fig. 1t). This observation suggests that the entropic influence of the carbon layer predominantly affects Na+ diffusion within the interlayer region, with a less direct impact on intracrystalline diffusion. The rapid diffusion at the interface facilitates the swift migration and even distribution of Na+ ions, creating additional migration sites for intracrystalline pathways towards the interface, thus fostering overall intracrystalline migration behavior.

Carbon dots (CDs) represent a novel frontier in the realm of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, distinguished by their special chemical, structural, and optical attributes [28]. Characterized by dimensions below 10 nm, these entities comprise a carbon core enveloped by a surface passivation layer [29]. Unlike their carbon-based counterparts, CDs boast a unique configuration and tunable functionality, owing to the diverse repertoire of carbon core and shell compositions and functional groups at their disposal [30]. The carbon core, serving as the foundational scaffold of CDs, is constituted of sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon atoms. Conversely, the surface passivation layer, akin to a protective shell, is replete with an array of functional groups or polymer chains rich in oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, among others, furnishing a solid basis for the formation of interfacial chemical bonds [31]. Therefore, CDs was chosen as the best precursor for the construction of carbon layers with multiple heteroatoms/defects coexisting, i.e. defect-configuration entropic carbon layers.

The NVP/C composite series were synthesized via a straightforward solid-phase ball-milling and sintering process. NVP/C, NVP/FC, and NVP/NSFC were obtained employing carbon dots (CDs, containing C, H, and O elements), fluorine-doped carbon dots (F-CDs, containing C, H, O and F elements), and nitrogen-sulfur-fluorine co-doped carbon dots (NSF-CDs, containing C, H, O, N, S and F elements) as the exclusive carbon sources, respectively. As shown in Figure S6, the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the NVP/C series reveal highly crystalline diffraction peaks corresponding to the rhombohedral phase (R-3c space group). The more detailed structural information was provided by the XRD Rietveld refinement ( Fig. 2a-c) and the consistent cell parameters across the NVP/C series suggest that the various carbon coatings do not alter the bulk crystal structure of NVP (Table S4). The distinctive features of the carbon layer within the NVP/C series samples were obtained by Raman spectra (Fig. 2d). The two strong peaks belonging to the D band (disordered carbon) and G band (graphitic carbon) are detected at 1347.1 and 1592.8 cm−1, respectively [32]. A gradual increase in the ID/IG ratio of NVP/C (0.910), NVP/FC (0.957), and NVP/NSFC (0.976) was observed, indicating an increase in the degree of disorder/defects in the carbon layer structure, which proves the successful doping of N/S/F heteroatoms into the carbon layer and the presence of carbon layer defects [33,34]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to analyze the chemical structure of the NVP/C series (Figure S7). The peaks observed at 579 and 1053 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of the P-O bonds, while unsymmetrical stretching vibrations of the PO4 tetrahedra are identified at 1184 cm−1. Furthermore, the peaks observed at 631 cm−1 signify vibrations corresponding to the V3+-O2- bonds within the VO6 octahedra [35]. The carbon content of the NVP/C series was obtained via thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The carbon-based coating content of NVP/C, NVP/FC, and NVP/NSFC are about 6.79 %, 5.08 %, and 4.55 %, respectively (Figure S8). Elemental analysis tests (EA) quantified the C, N, and S content in the composite samples (Table S5). The chemical environment of the NVP/C series was further probed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. Full spectra detected signals for each element (Figure S9). The C 1 s orbital high-resolution spectrum in Fig. 2e reveals the presence of C-N/C-S/C-F bonds, confirming the successful doping of N, S, and F elements in the carbon layer [36]. Additionally, N 1 s orbitals of NVP/NSFC indicated pyridinic N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic N (401.2 eV), and graphitized N (402.9 eV) forms (Figure S11a) [37]. The S 2p (Figure S11b) and F 1 s (Figure S10 and Figure S11c) fine spectra illustrate the form of bonding of C-S/C-F [38,39]. V 2p orbital high-resolution spectra (Fig. 2f) show characteristic V3+ peaks at 517.3 (V 2p3/2) and 524.4 eV (V 2p1/2) in all samples [40]. The four-probe test was carried out to understand the difference in electronic conductivity for NVP/C series, indicating that NVP/NSFC has a much higher conductivity (≈5.6×10−2 S cm−1, Table S6) than the other two samples.

The morphology of the NVP/C series was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). NVP/C is characterized by large, highly agglomerated lumpy particles with smaller particles adhering to the outer surface (Fig. 2 g and Figure S12a-b). NVP/FC displays an internal porous morphology due to fluorine gas escape during sintering, resulting in more sparse and finely crushed particles (Fig. 2 h and Figure S12c-d). Conversely, NVP/NSFC demonstrates homogeneous and compact particles with a smooth outer surface, suggesting uniform carbon layer formation on the cathode surface (Fig. 2i and Figure S12e-f). This layer acts as a conductive physical protection shield, enhancing mechanical stability during high-rate cycling [41]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to examine the microstructure of the NVP/C series (Figure S13-S15). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) revealed a carbon layer approximately 4 nm thick in all three samples (Figs. 2j-2l). Clear lattice fringes corresponding to the (211), (300), and (104) crystallographic planes of NVP are observed with spacings of 0.285 nm, 0.253 nm, and 0.440 nm in NVP/C, NVP/FC, and NVP/NSFC, respectively (Fig. 2 m, 2o, and 2q), which are consistent with the Rietveld refinement confirmation of structures. Selected area electron diffraction (SEAD) analysis further confirmed the physical phase of the NVP/C series (Fig. 2 n, 2p, and 2r). Additionally, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) verified the homogeneous distribution of elements in the particles (Figs. 2s-2u).

To evaluate the enhanced sodium storage performance facilitated by the defect configuration entropy of the carbon layer on the NVP cathode, half-cells of the NVP/C series were assembled for electrochemical assessment in the voltage window of 2.3–4.0 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed to analyze the electrochemical behavior of the NVP/C series. As depicted in Fig. 3a-c, a distinct pair of redox peaks is evident across all electrodes, situated around 3.4 V, corresponding to the V4+/V3+ redox pair [42]. Additionally, a minor reduction peak is observed near 3.2 V, attributed to the alteration in the local redox environment induced by the transfer of Na (1) to the Na (2) site [43]. Notably, the NVP/NSFC electrode exhibits the sharpest redox peaks across sweep rates from 0.1 mV s−1 to 1.0 mV s−1, indicative of superior redox kinetics. Moreover, at the sweep rate of 1.0 mV s−1, a minimal potential difference between the oxidation and reduction peaks is observed for the NVP/NSFC electrode (0.366 V), which indicates minimized electrochemical polarization and improved electrochemical reaction kinetics. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) collectively underscored the exceptional electrochemical kinetic performance of the NVP/NSFC electrode. As depicted in Fig. 3d, the charge transfer impedance (Rct) of NVP/C, NVP/FC, and NVP/NSFC are 397.8, 253.5, and 203.9 Ω, respectively. The NVP/NSFC electrode exhibits remarkable performance in comparison to NVP/C and NVP/FC, showcasing superior sodium ion diffusion kinetics, and NVP/C displays the least favorable electrochemical kinetics (Figure S16).

Subsequently, the rate performance of the NVP/C series was estimated. As shown in Fig. 3e, NVP/NSFC surpasses higher than NVP/FC and NVP/C at all the current densities. At various rates of 0.5 C, 1 C, 2 C, 5 C, 10 C, 20 C, 50 C, 70 C, and 100 C (1 C=117 mA g−1), NVP/NSFC exhibits discharge capacities of 117.8, 111, 105.8, 103.2, 101.5, 100.5, 100.1, 98.2, and 98.1 mAh g−1, respectively. Even upon returning to 0.5 C, it retains a high capacity of 115.6 mAh g−1 (98.1 % retention rate compared to the initial capacity), which demonstrates outstanding rate performance. In contrast, NVP/FC reveals good discharge capacity at lower currents but suffers rapid capacity decay at higher rates, indicating limited performance due to electronic/ionic conductivity constraints. NVP/C exhibits the poorest rate performance, consistent with its inferior electronic and sodium ion diffusion kinetics. Notably, the outstanding ultra-fast charging performance of NVP/NSFC electrode is demonstrated, and a high capacity of 88.9 mAh g−1 (Figure S17 and Figure S18) is maintained at an ultra-high rate of 200 C (∼13 s per charge process). Due to the innovative design of the defective configuration entropy carbon layer, NVP/NSFC facilitates rapid ion and electron transmission by reducing the Na+ diffusion energy barrier and enhancing electronic conductivity. This breakthrough lays the foundation for achieving exceptional rate performance. Notably, NVP/NSFC cathodes exhibit significant advantages over previously reported NVP/C materials (Fig. 3f, Table S7).

Furthermore, the cycling stability of the NVP/C series cathodes was comprehensively evaluated. After 500 cycles at a low rate of 1 C (Figure S19-S20), the NVP/C, NVP/FC, and NVP/NSFC electrodes exhibit capacity retention of 85.4 %, 90.7 %, and 99.7 %, respectively. Notably, NVP/NSFC maintains a remarkable capacity retention of 98.7 % after 3000 cycles at 10 C (Figure S21), which significantly outperforms NVP/C (69.4 %) and NVP/FC (83.8 %). The gap between electrochemical reversible capacity and cycling stability is further magnified with increasing current density. Impressively, even after ultra-long cycling of 10000 cycles at 50 C (Figure S22), ultra-high capacity retention of 94.9 % is achieved by NVP/NSFC, which is superior to NVP/C (46.7 %) and NVP/FC (57.7 %). Under more demanding conditions of 100 C, NVP/NSFC is stably cycled for 5000 cycles with a capacity retention rate of 90.2 % (Fig. 3g). Even at ultra-high rates of 200 C, it still achieves a reversible capacity of 65.6 mAh g−1 after 1600 cycles (Figure S23), demonstrating exceptional stability under extreme cycling conditions, much higher than those of other NVP/C materials. The exceptional cycling stability of NVP/NSFC is attributed to the ingenious design of the defect configuration entropy-driven carbon layer. This strategy effectively suppresses the formation of vanadium vacancies while simultaneously improving the bonding strength of the V-O/V-C interfaces, which results in a dual effect of improved structural and interfacial stability. To fully evaluate the wide temperature range suitability of the designed NVP/C series, the ability to withstand extreme operating environments from −30 °C to 60 °C was investigated. The capacity retention and cycle life of cathodes in low-temperature are predominantly influenced by both external and internal factors. External factors include the rise in electrolyte viscosity at low temperatures, diminishing electrical conductivity and augmenting the migration impedance of Na+. Internally, slow mass and charge transfer processes within the cathode bulk under low temperatures hinder electrode kinetics and escalate polarization [32,44]. The designed NVP/NSFC cathode exhibits a dual enhancement in electronic and ionic conductivity, resulting in exceptional low-temperature performance. The cycling performance of the NVP/C series electrodes at −20 ℃ is shown in Fig. 3h, a reversible capacity of 71.3 mAh g−1 with a capacity retention of 69.6 % after 300 cycles (1 C, 0.101 % capacity decay per cycle) is achieved. In contrast, the initial capacities of NVP/C and NVP/FC are only 42.4 mAh g−1 and 61.1 mAh g−1, respectively. Remarkably, normal operation of the NVP/NSFC electrode is maintained even at −30 ℃ (Figure S24 and Figure S25), with a reversible capacity of approximately 80 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C. In comparison, the stability of the cathode-electrolyte interface (CEI) and cathode structures faces significant challenges in high-temperature environments. The dissolution of CEI membranes and degradation of cathode structures under elevated temperatures pose substantial threats to the thermal stability of battery [45]. The defect-configuration entropy-driven carbon layer plays a crucial role in enhancing interfacial stability by acting as a robust physical protection shield, effectively mitigating cathode structure decomposition and minimizing side reactions with the electrolyte. Consequently, the NVP/NSFC electrode demonstrates superior initial capacity and cycling stability (104.2 mAh g−1, 92.9 % capacity retention after 1500 cycles, 0.0047 % capacity decay per cycle) at 60 ℃, while NVP/C and NVP/FC electrodes experience rapid degradation (Fig. 3i). In summary, the NVP/NSFC cathode under the effect of defect-configuration entropy carbon layer exhibits fast charging performance and stability in the full temperature region, which is promising for a wide range of application.

In-situ XRD tests were conducted to explore the crystal structure evolution of the NVP/C series (Figure S26). Following two uninterrupted charge-discharge cycles, all samples exhibit a characteristic two-phase, two-electron reaction: Na3V2(PO4)3 → NaV2(PO4)3 + 2Na++ 2e-. The diffraction peaks at 23.88°, 28.84°, 31.68°, and 32.14°, corresponding to the (113), (024), (211), and (116) crystal planes, respectively, show strong reversibility ( Fig. 4a-b, Figure S27a-b, and Figure S28a-b). The diffraction peaks of Na3V2(PO4)3 phase gradually weaken during the charging process while the diffraction peaks of NaV2(PO4)3 phase appear and gradually enhance. Conversely, during discharging, the diffraction peaks representing the NaV2(PO4)3 phase weaken and the Na3V2(PO4)3 phase reverting. The difference in peak displacement between the diffraction peaks of the characteristic (113) crystal plane after two cycles served as a metric to assess the electrochemical reversibility and structural stability of the NVP/C series electrodes. At the end of two discharge/charge cycles, the remarkable reversibility of the NVP/NSFC electrodes is exhibited with the peak displacement difference of 0° (Fig. 4c-d), underscoring its robust electrochemical reversibility and structural stability. This elucidates the excellent rate performance and cycling stability of the NVP/NSFC electrode from a structural evolution perspective. In contrast, the NVP/FC electrode displays a displacement difference of 0.02° after the first discharge to 2.3 V (Figure S28c-d), while the NVP/C electrode exhibits a comparable displacement difference of 0.02° at the end of both discharge cycles (Figure S27c-d), which suggests inferior structural stability.

The Hard X-ray absorption spectroscopy was employed to delve deeper into the charge compensation mechanism during the charge/discharge of the NVP/C series, and the X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) was used to obtain the electronic structure information of the active element vanadium (V). Various states of charge (SOC) were engineered to evaluate the NVP/C series samples, including pristine, 4.0 V charged state (C 4.0 V), 2.3 V discharged state (D 2.3 V), and post 1000 cycles at 10 C (after cycling). The V K-edge XANES patterns of NVP/C, NVP/FC, and NVP/NSFC in the initial state are depicted in Fig. 4e. All samples display similar patterns, indicating analogous average localized electronic structures. This finding is in agreement with previous studies of Na3V2(PO4)3 material and suggests an average valence state of 3+ of V [46]. However, it is noteworthy that the oscillation strengths at the pre-edge of NVP/FC and NVP/NSFC are reduced compared to NVP/C upon the introduction of heteroatoms and vacancies in the carbon layer, which implies a reduction in lattice distortion of the VO6 octahedra of NVP and enhanced structural stability [47,48]. When charging to the highest state of 4.0 V (Fig. 4f), a gradual shift to lower energies from NVP/NSFC to NVP/C is observed along the absorption edge of the V K edge, which denotes a gradual decrease in the valence state. In other words, at the same electrochemical charge state, NVP/NSFC exhibits the maximum participation in the oxidation reaction (V3+→V4+). When discharging to 2.3 V (Fig. 4g), the gradual increase in valence from NVP/NSFC to NVP/C is evidenced by the gradual shift of the absorption edge towards higher energies, suggesting that the electronic state of NVP/NSFC is maximally restored to the initial (V4+→V3+) [49,50]. Consequently, NVP/NSFC undergoes a more complete and deeper electrochemical redox reaction for the highest reversible capacity compared to the other samples, consistent with the trend of the electrochemical performance, while NVP/C undergoes the least complete electrochemical reaction. The cycling stability of the samples was further assessed by scrutinizing the disparities in the electronic states of the V elements, as depicted in Fig. 4h. Analysis of the absorption edge of the NVP/C series after cycling reveals that NVP/NSFC exhibits the lowest electronic state, which explains its excellent electrochemical reversibility after long cycling from the perspective of charge compensation. This is further confirmed by the R spacing curves of V K-edge extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS), where the increased radius is observed for the NVP/NSFC sample (Fig. 4i) [51]. Furthermore, by examining the first coordination shell layer of V-O (Fig. 4j) that the wavelet transforms of the k3-weighted EXAFS profiles have also been applied to verify the electronic states after cycling (Fig. 4k-m), the results show that NVP/NSFC displays the most excellent electrochemical reversibility [52].

The NVP/C series was further explored from the perspective of interfacial chemistry. Initially, the electrolyte wettability of the samples was evaluated through dynamic contact angle tests. Improved electrolyte wettability serves as a critical avenue for enhancing the degree and efficiency of electrochemical electrode/electrolyte interfacial interaction and further significantly affects the electrochemical energy storage performance of the electrodes [53]. It is observed that NVP/NSFC exhibits superior wettability compared to NVP/C and NVP/FC when the same volume of electrolyte is added dropwise under identical conditions ( Fig. 5a-c). The enhanced wettability of NVP/NSFC and NVP/FC comes from the introduction of electronegative heteroatoms that interact with the electrolyte [54]. Then the morphological differences of NVP/C series after cycling were investigated by using TEM. As shown in Figure S29, a loose CEI with uneven thickness after cycling is formed on the surface of the carbon layer of NVP/C, the CEI film on the surface of the carbon layer of NVP/FC shows a uniform thickness but is not flat enough (Figure S30), and then a thin but uniform and dense CEI film is formed on NVP/NSFC (Figure S31). To explore the composition of the CEI and its correlation with the electrochemical performance of the cathodes, the in-depth Ar+ sputtering for 0 s, 50 s, 150 s, and 250 s was conducted on the NVP/C series electrodes after cycling. As depicted in Fig. 5d-i, similar components were identified in the CEI of all samples, encompassing organic constituents (RONa, RCONa, etc.) and inorganic constituents (NaF, Na2CO3, etc.) [55]. However, the analysis of the F 1 s orbital and the percentage distribution of key constituents (C, O, F, Na) in the CEI reveals notable distinctions (Figure S32). The CEI of NVP/C exhibits the lowest proportion of NaF compared to NVP/FC and NVP/NSFC, while displaying a higher concentration of oxygen, indicating an organic-dominated CEI formation. In contrast, a higher content of NaF is detected for the CEI of NVP/NSFC. Renowned for its efficacy as a Na+ ion conductor and electrical insulator, NaF fosters efficient Na+ diffusion pathways, fortifies interface mechanical integrity within the CEI, and alleviates electrolyte decomposition [8,56]. Consequently, the NaF-enriched CEI of NVP/NSFC serves to suppress interfacial side reactions, enhancing electrode kinetics and yielding superior electrochemical performance [57]. For further compositional analysis of the electrode surface, time-of-flight secondary-ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS) was employed due to its ultrahigh chemical selectivity and sub-nanometer surface sensitivity [58]. The results synergistically verified the cyclic by-products and spatial distribution of the three cathodes, and the NVP/NSFC cathode surface with a uniformly distributed NaF fraction was re-emphasized (Fig. 5j-l). The NaF-rich interface is an important reason for the excellent stability of NVP/NSFC over the full-temperature region.

To assess the feasibility of NVP/NSFC, the full cell (NVP/NSFC ‖ HC) employing hard carbon (HC) as the anode and NVP/NSFC as the cathode was constructed ( Fig. 6a). As illustrated in Fig. 6b, the full cell successfully illuminated the LED light board. The critical influence of electrolyte engineering on full-cell performance has been evident in our prior work [59]. Thus, employing ether electrolyte, the GCD curves of the NVP/NSFC cathode and HC anode are shown in Fig. 6c. The NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cell was initially evaluated for its rate performance (Fig. 6e), delivering discharge specific capacities of 102, 100.6, 99.4, 97.7, and 94.7 mAh g−1 at 1 C, 2 C, 5 C, 10 C, and 20 C, respectively, indicative of excellent ionic/electronic conductivity. The energy density versus power density of the NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cell (based on cathode mass) at different rates was calculated in Figure S33, which achieves an energy density of 226.6 Wh Kg−1 even at a very large power density of 5610.45 W Kg−1 (20 C), demonstrating the practical value of the NVP/NSFC. Subsequently, outstanding performance is observed at 5 C for cycling stability tests, with an initial specific capacity of 102.2 mAh g−1 and a remarkable capacity retention of 93.1 % after 1400 cycles (Fig. 6d). Even at a higher rate of 10 C, an initial specific capacity of 100.8 mAh g−1 is achieved, with a capacity retention rate of 81.4 % after 1500 cycles (Fig. 6g). These findings underscore the superior electrochemical performance of the NVP/NSFC electrode in full cells, presenting clear advantages regardless of capacity or cycling stability over the currently reported NVP/C electrode (Fig. 6f, Table S8).

In conclusion, inspired by the entropy effect cathode design principle, a defect-configured entropy-strengthened carbon layer derived from functionalized CDs was constructed on the NVP surface. The presence of a heterogeneous interface influenced by defect configuration entropy concurrently enhances the stability of the bulk phase and interface of NVP cathode, substantially lowers the Na+ diffusion energy barrier, and enhances electronic conductivity, thereby achieving exceptional ion/electron diffusion dynamics. In addition, the increased electrolyte wettability of the defective configuration entropy interface and the induction of enrichment of the NaF-rich CEI further promote the electrochemical performances. The devised NVP/NSFC cathode demonstrates outstanding rate performance and cycle stability, retaining an ultra-high specific capacity of 88.9 mAh g−1 at 200 C (∼13 s per charge process) and maintaining stable cycling for 5000 cycles at an ultra-high rate of 100 C, with a capacity retention rate of 90.2 % post cycling. Furthermore, its performance spanned a wide-operational temperature of −30 °C to 60 °C, retaining 69.6 % capacity after 300 stable cycles at −20 ℃ (1 C, 0.101 % capacity decay per cycle), and 0.0047 % capacity decay per cycle at 60 ℃ for 10 C. Lastly, the NVP/NSFC ‖ HC full cell also presents notable performance advantages, retaining a high reversible capacity of 94.7 mAh g−1 at 20 C, while maintaining a capacity retention rate of 93.1 % after stable cycling for 1400 cycles at 5 C. This defect-configuration entropy design strategy enables the achievement of high reversible capacity, cycle durability, and excellent fast charging capability in a wide-operational temperature, thereby enhancing the practical value of polyanionic cathodes.

Xiaobo Ji: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yujin Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hongshuai Hou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yu Mei: Software, Investigation. Huaxin Liu: Methodology. Hanrui Ding: Investigation. Yujie Huang: Methodology. Xue Zhong: Software. Zhenglei Geng: Investigation. Zidong He: Software. Wentao Deng: Resources. Guoqiang Zou: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22379165, 52261135632, 52074359), the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2021RC3014), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of the Central South University (2024ZZTS0200).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2024.110107.
